**PART 1**   
Answer the following before running the simulation:

1\. What is an agent in an Agent-Based Model?

2\. What is the difference between:  
o global variables  
o species variables?

3\. What does this expression mean?  
student mean\_of each.attention

4\. What happens if attention continuously decreases without a break?

**PART 2 — Run the Base Model**  
**Step 1**  
Run the provided model.  
![][image1]

**Step 2**  
Observe:  
• Student movement  
• Color changes  
• Monitor values  
![][image2]

**Step 3**  
Open the generated file: classroom\_data.csv

**PART 3 — Data Observation Tabl**

| Metric | Value |
| :---- | :---- |
| Average Attention | 0.8000000000000003 |
| Average Performance | 1.0 |
| High Attention Count | 25 |
| Numbers or breaks occured | 2 |

**PART 4 — Guided Code Analysis**  
![][image3]  
**Questions:**  
1\. Does attention increase faster? Yes  
2\. Does performance grow faster? Yes  
3\. Is the system more stable? Yes

**Activity 2: Attention Decay Rate**  
Original:  
attention \<- max(0.0, attention \- 0.02);  
**Task:**  
Change decay rate to:  
0.05

**Observe:**  
• Does attention collapse? No  
• Does performance still improve? Yes  
Explain why.  
Even though it’s faster it didn't collapse, The performance improved and became faster.  
![][image4]  
![][image5]

**Activity 3: Performance Growth Condition**

![][image6]

**Questions:**  
• Does performance improve slower? No  
• What does this represent in real classroom settings? That students are very active if it were in a real classroom setting the students move, interactive, and are more active. 

**PART 5 — Experiment: Class Size Impact (30 minutes)**

| Students | avg attention | avg performance |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| 10 | 0.7842271172502727 | 0.98 |
| 25 | 0.23422711725027234 | 0.98 |
| 60 | 0.6842271172502723 | 0.98 |
| 100 | 0.4842271172502725 | 0.98 |

**Analysis Questions:**  
1\. Does increasing class size affect average attention? Yes  
2\. Does mobility create more randomness? Yes  
3\. Is emergent behavior visible? Yes

**PART 6 — Data Analysis Task (Optional Python)**  
**![][image7]**  
![][image8]

**3\. Classroom Model \- Break Cycle Analysis**

**Intentional Break Cycle (Working Correctly)**

The model toggles the is\_break flag every 30 cycles, alternating between class and break periods.

gaml  
if (cycle mod 30 \= 0\) {  
    is\_break \<- \!is\_break;  
}

Break Cycle Timeline:

| Break Cycle | State | Description |
| ----- | ----- | ----- |
| 0 \- 29 | is\_break \= false | Class in session |
| 30 \- 59 | is\_break \= true | Break active |
| 60 \- 89 | is\_break \= false | Class in session |
| 90 \- 119 | is\_break \= true | Break active |

**Issues / Unintended Break Cycle Behaviors**

**1\. Cycle 0 Double-Trigger**

At cycle 0, the toggle condition fires immediately, flipping is\_break to true on the very first tick. This means students start in a break rather than in class.

Fix:

gaml  
if (cycle \> 0 and cycle mod 30 \= 0\) {  
    is\_break \<- \!is\_break;  
}

**2\. CSV Rewrite Logic \- Cycle 0 State Logged Incorrectly**

The CSV rewrite flag only triggers on cycle 0\. Because of the double-trigger bug above, the initial row logs is\_break \= true, which does not reflect the intended starting state.

**3\. Attention Floor/Ceiling Asymmetry Creates a Slow Bleed Break Cycle**

The attention recovery during breaks is much stronger than the decay during class periods. Over 30 cycles, students gain significantly more attention than they lose, causing attention to trend toward the maximum over time.

| Break Cycle | Change Per Cycle | Total Over 30 Cycles |
| ----- | ----- | ----- |
| Break (+) | \+0.05 | \+1.50 (capped at 1.0) |
| Class (-) | \-0.02 | \-0.60 |

Consider increasing class-period decay for more realistic attention dynamics:

gaml  
attention \<- max(0.0, attention \- 0.04);

**4\. Students Can Walk Out of Bounds**

The movement reflex applies no boundary check, so students drift outside the 100x100 world indefinitely.

Fix:

gaml  
float new\_x \<- min(world\_size, max(0.0, location.x \+ step\_size \* cos(angle)));  
float new\_y \<- min(world\_size, max(0.0, location.y \+ step\_size \* sin(angle)));  
location \<- {new\_x, new\_y};  
---

**Break Cycle Summary**

| Break Cycle | Period | Status |
| ----- | ----- | ----- |
| Break toggle | Every 30 cycles | Works, but off-by-one at cycle 0 |
| Attention recovery | Per-tick during break | Works, but overpowered |
| Performance gain | When attention \> 0.6 | Correct |
| CSV logging | Every cycle | Initial state logged incorrectly |
| Spatial bounds | N/A | No boundary enforcement |

4\. Compute correlation between attention and performance  
![][image9]  
**Question:**  
Is performance strongly dependent on attention?

- Performance only increases by 0.01 when attention exceeds 0.6 and never decreases, so no, it is not strongly dependent.

**PART 7 — Critical Thinking Questions**

**1\. Why does performance only increase when attention \> 0.6?**

Because that is the threshold hardcoded in the model it represents the assumption that only students paying sufficient attention (above 60%) actually absorb and retain learning. Below that, no learning is recorded.

**2\. Is this model deterministic or stochastic?**

Stochastic. Three sources of randomness exist in the code:

* `attention <- rnd(1.0)` — random initial attention per student  
* `mobility <- rnd(1.0)` — random initial mobility per student  
* `angle <- rnd(360.0)` — random movement direction each cycle

**3\. What real-world classroom factors are missing?**

* Teacher behavior and teaching quality  
* Student fatigue accumulating over time  
* Peer interaction and group dynamics  
* Individual learning differences and disabilities  
* Subject difficulty varying over time  
* External distractions (phone, noise)  
* Motivation and intrinsic interest

**4\. How would peer influence affect the system?**

Currently students are completely independent — their attention and performance update with no awareness of neighbors. Adding peer influence would mean a student's attention shifts toward the average of nearby students, creating cluster effects where high-attention groups pull each other up and low-attention groups drag each other down. You could implement it by adding a neighbor-averaging term to the `update_attention` reflex.

**PART 8 — Advanced Extension Tasks (Choose One)**

Option C: Fatigue Model

Add:

• If attention \< 0.3 for 10 cycles → performance decreases

model Classroom  
global {  
   int nb\_students **\<-** 25;  
   float world\_size **\<-** 100.0;  
   bool is\_break **\<-** false;  
   float avg\_attention **\-\>** { **student** **mean\_of** *each*.**attention** };  
   float avg\_performance **\-\>** { **student** **mean\_of** *each*.**performance** };  
   int high\_attention\_count **\-\>** { **student** **count** (*each*.**attention** **\>** 0.7) };  
   init {  
       create **student** **number**: **nb\_students** {  
           **location** **\<-** {**rnd**(**world\_size**), **rnd**(**world\_size**)};  
       }  
   }  
   reflex classroom\_cycle {  
       if (**cycle** **mod** 15 **\=** 0) {  
           **is\_break** **\<-** \! **is\_break**;  
       }  
       save \[  
           **cycle**,  
           **avg\_attention**,  
           **avg\_performance**,  
           **is\_break**,  
           **high\_attention\_count**  
       \]  
       **to**: "classroom\_data.csv"  
       **format**: "csv"  
       **rewrite**: (**cycle** **\=** 0) ? true : false  
       **header**: true;  
   }  
}  
species student {  
   float attention **\<-** **rnd**(1.0);  
   float performance **\<-** 0.5;  
   float mobility **\<-** **rnd**(1.0);  
   int low\_attention\_streak **\<-** 0;  
   rgb color **\<-** \#*blue*;  
   reflex update\_attention {  
       if (**is\_break**) {  
           **attention** **\<-** **min**(1.0, **attention** **\+** 0.05);  
       } else {  
           **attention** **\<-** **max**(0.0, **attention** **\-** 0.05);  
       }  
       if (**attention** **\>** 0.8) {  
           **performance** **\<-** **min**(1.0, **performance** **\+** 0.01);  
       }  
       if (**attention** **\<** 0.3) {  
           **low\_attention\_streak** **\<-** **low\_attention\_streak** **\+** 1;  
       } else {  
           **low\_attention\_streak** **\<-** 0;  
       }  
       if (**low\_attention\_streak** **\>=** 10) {  
           **performance** **\<-** **max**(0.0, **performance** **\-** 0.01);  
       }  
       if (**attention** **\>** 0.7) {  
           **color** **\<-** \#*pink*;  
       } else if (**attention** **\>** 0.4) {  
           **color** **\<-** \#*yellow*;  
       } else {  
           **color** **\<-** \#*blue*;  
       }  
   }  
   reflex move {  
       float step\_size **\<-** **mobility** **\*** 2;  
       float angle **\<-** **rnd**(360.0);  
       **location** **\<-** **location** **\+** {**step\_size** **\*** **cos**(**angle**), **step\_size** **\*** **sin**(**angle**)};  
   }  
   aspect base {  
       draw **circle**(2) **color**: **color**;  
   }  
}  
experiment classroom\_simulation **type:** **gui** {  
   parameter "Initial number of students:" **var:** **nb\_students** **min**: 10 **max**: 100;  
   output {  
       display main\_display **type:** **2d** {  
           species **student** **aspect:** **base**;  
       }  
       monitor "Average Attention" **value:** **avg\_attention**;  
       monitor "Average Performance" **value:** **avg\_performance**;  
       monitor "High Attention Count" **value:** **high\_attention\_count**;  
   }  
}
